result.markdown:
---
title: "Rasters"
---

# Getting started
___

**Packages needed for this tutorial**:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(terra)       # working with rasters in R (replaces raster/rgdal)
library(tidyverse)   # installs sweeps of packages
library(sf)          # working with sf objects in R
library(tidyterra)   # ggplot2 integration for terra objects
library(marmap)      # access global topography data
```
:::


::: {.callout-note}
This chapter uses **terra**, which replaced the older **raster** and **rgdal** packages. The API is similar -- differences are noted throughout. For the full background, see the [Spatial data concepts](spatial_data.qmd#the-r-ecosystem-for-spatial-data) chapter.
:::

# Raster data model

The raster data model was introduced in the [Spatial data concepts](spatial_data.qmd) chapter. As a brief recap: raster data represents continuous fields as a regular grid of equally-sized cells. Each cell holds one or more values, and the grid is defined by its **extent**, **resolution**, and **origin**. The coordinate reference system is a fourth required property.

::: {.callout-note}
If you need to revisit the conceptual background — the difference between continuous and categorical rasters, multi-layer rasters, or irregular grid types — see the [Spatial data concepts](spatial_data.qmd#raster-data-model) section.
:::


# Raster classes in R

The **terra** package provides a single class, **SpatRaster**, which handles both single-layer and multi-layer rasters. This replaces the three separate classes of the old **raster** package (`RasterLayer`, `RasterBrick`, `RasterStack`).

For vector data, **terra** provides the **SpatVector** class, though in this course we mainly use **sf** objects for vector data. The two are interoperable: `vect()` converts an sf object to a SpatVector, and `st_as_sf()` goes the other direction.

# Reading rasters into R

There are many different formats for raster data. The **terra** package uses the external library **GDAL** to read them. You can get the list of all possible formats here:

- https://gdal.org/drivers/raster/index.html

GeoTIFF (`.tif` or `.tiff`) and ASCII grid (`.asc`) are two of the most common formats.

Reading rasters with **terra** uses the `rast()` function. Let's try it with a raster with bottom temperature around Iceland. These were compiled using data from NISE (Norwegian Iceland Seas Experiment) project.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Minimum temperature
mintemp <- rast("./data/Iceland_minbtemp.tif")

# Maximum temperature
maxtemp <- rast("./data/Iceland_maxbtemp.tif")

# And a bit of data off the Faroe Islands
far_temp <- rast("./data/Faroes_minbtemp.tif")

# Multi-layer rasters: pass a vector of paths, or use c()
files <- c("./data/Iceland_minbtemp.tif",
           "./data/Iceland_maxbtemp.tif")

temps <- rast(files)

class(mintemp)    # SpatRaster
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "SpatRaster"
attr(,"package")
[1] "terra"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
nlyr(mintemp)     # number of layers (replaces nlayers())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 1
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
class(temps)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "SpatRaster"
attr(,"package")
[1] "terra"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
nlyr(temps)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
names(temps) # By default the name of the files
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Iceland_minbtemp" "Iceland_maxbtemp"
```


:::
:::


We can do a quick plot using terra's built-in plot method.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(mintemp)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(temps)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-2.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Raster metadata

Printing a SpatRaster shows its metadata, including dimensions, resolution, extent, coordinate reference system, and value range.

To get the metadata directly from the file without fully reading it into memory, use `describe()`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
describe("./data/Iceland_minbtemp.tif")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}


:::
:::


The coordinate reference system can be extracted (and set) with `crs()`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
crs(mintemp)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "PROJCRS[\"unnamed\",\n    BASEGEOGCRS[\"WGS 84\",\n        DATUM[\"World Geodetic System 1984\",\n            ELLIPSOID[\"WGS 84\",6378137,298.257223563,\n                LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]]],\n        PRIMEM[\"Greenwich\",0,\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433]],\n        ID[\"EPSG\",4326]],\n    CONVERSION[\"Lambert Azimuthal Equal Area\",\n        METHOD[\"Lambert Azimuthal Equal Area\",\n            ID[\"EPSG\",9820]],\n        PARAMETER[\"Latitude of natural origin\",69,\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8801]],\n        PARAMETER[\"Longitude of natural origin\",-4,\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8802]],\n        PARAMETER[\"False easting\",0,\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8806]],\n        PARAMETER[\"False northing\",0,\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8807]]],\n    CS[Cartesian,2],\n        AXIS[\"(E)\",east,\n            ORDER[1],\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]],\n        AXIS[\"(N)\",north,\n            ORDER[2],\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]]]"
```


:::
:::


The `ext()` function provides an object of class *SpatExtent* with the ranges of the horizontal and vertical coordinates:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ext(mintemp)    # replaces extent()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
SpatExtent : -1050225.89572246, -300225.89572246, -699984.32110574, -19984.32110574 (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
st_bbox(mintemp)   # an bbox object compatible with sf
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       xmin        ymin        xmax        ymax 
-1050225.90  -699984.32  -300225.90   -19984.32 
```


:::
:::


The values in the extent object are in the coordinate reference system of the raster.  Sometimes is useful to get an (approximate) extent in latitude and longitude



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
project(ext(mintemp),
        from=crs(mintemp),
        to="EPSG:4326")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
SpatExtent : -28.6592355818319, -9.84370073548541, 61.2002918802469, 68.658417118001 (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
```


:::
:::



# Summary statistics

The function `global()` computes summary statistics of a *SpatRaster* object. This replaces `cellStats()` from the old **raster** package.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
global(mintemp, mean, na.rm = TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                     mean
Iceland_minbtemp 2.672053
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
global(temps, mean, na.rm = TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                     mean
Iceland_minbtemp 2.672053
Iceland_maxbtemp 3.320894
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(mintemp)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 Iceland_minbtemp 
 Min.   :-0.9983  
 1st Qu.: 0.2705  
 Median : 2.6790  
 Mean   : 2.6713  
 3rd Qu.: 4.5784  
 Max.   : 8.6031  
 NAs    :19742    
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
hist(mintemp)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Rasters with categorical (factor) data

SpatRaster objects can store logical, integer, continuous or categorical data.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Create classification matrix
cm <- matrix(c(
  -2, 2, 1,
  2,  4, 2,
  4, 10, 3),
  ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)

# Reclassify: replaces reclassify()
temp_reclass <- classify(mintemp, cm)

is.factor(temp_reclass)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] FALSE
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(temp_reclass)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Make a factor raster
temp_factor <- as.factor(temp_reclass)
is.factor(temp_factor)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] TRUE
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Add category labels to the raster attribute table
levels(temp_factor) <- data.frame(ID = 1:3,
                                  category = c("cold", "mild", "warm"))

plot(temp_factor)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-2.png){width=672}
:::
:::


With **tidyterra** we can plot categorical rasters with ggplot naturally:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot() +
  geom_spatraster(data = temp_factor) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("blue", "lightblue", "red"),
                    na.value = "transparent",
                    name = "Category") 
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



# Manipulation of raster objects

## Extracting values from a raster

To extract values from a *SpatRaster*, we use the bracket `[` operator or the `extract()` function. Note that the **terra** `extract()` lives in the **terra** namespace, so if **tidyverse** is also loaded you may need to call it explicitly as `terra::extract()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mintemp[239]          # Cell ID = 239
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  Iceland_minbtemp
1       -0.4235116
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
mintemp[20, 50]       # Row 20, column 50
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  Iceland_minbtemp
1        0.2513673
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Same as...
cell <- cellFromRowCol(mintemp, 20, 50)
mintemp[cell]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  Iceland_minbtemp
1        0.2513673
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
mintemp[20:25, 50:55] # Submatrix
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   Iceland_minbtemp
1       0.251367331
2       0.197344482
3       0.129703626
4       0.059670381
5      -0.012719928
6      -0.086739257
7       0.236171514
8       0.177205890
9       0.112170070
10      0.044947170
11     -0.024629701
12     -0.094837427
13      0.222400844
14      0.161106348
15      0.097580016
16      0.032607112
17     -0.034178130
18     -0.102023244
19      0.207514465
20      0.147138044
21      0.085331626
22      0.022095216
23     -0.042773183
24     -0.109881550
25      0.193299651
26      0.134642586
27      0.074557401
28      0.013044102
29     -0.051073473
30     -0.118012875
31      0.180556059
32      0.123616993
33      0.065251671
34      0.005331486
35     -0.059210896
36     -0.125822097
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# With drop = FALSE we get a new SpatRaster instead of a vector
rst <- mintemp[20:25, 50:55, drop = FALSE]
plot(rst)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Crop using an extent object
ext_obj <- ext(c(-944011, -784505, -278260, -160260))
rst <- crop(mintemp, ext_obj)
plot(rst)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-2.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Replacing values


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
rst <- mintemp[20:25, 50:55, drop = FALSE]

rst[4, 2] <- rst[4, 2] + 30
rst[1:2]  <- rst[1:2]  + 10
plot(rst)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Masks

We can mask a raster with another raster that shares the same geometry. Cells with `NA` values in the mask are removed from the masked raster.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
rst <- mintemp[100:135, 50:85, drop = FALSE]

# Create an empty raster with the same geometry
my.mask <- rast(rst)
values(my.mask) <- 1   # Non-NA everywhere

set.seed(100)
cells <- sample(1:ncell(my.mask), 20)
my.mask[cells] <- NA

rst_masked <- mask(rst, my.mask)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(rst)
plot(rst_masked)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Extracting raster data with sf objects



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
bt_points <- read_sf("./data/ice_points.gpkg")
bt_lines  <- read_sf("./data/ice_lines.gpkg")
bt_poly   <- read_sf("./data/ice_polygon.gpkg")

plot(mintemp)
plot(bt_points, add = TRUE)
plot(bt_lines,  add = TRUE)
plot(bt_poly,   add = TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


We use `terra::extract()` to extract raster values using sf objects:

- For POINT geometry: values of the cells where the points are located.
- For LINESTRING geometry: values of the cells touched by the line.
- For POLYGON geometry: values of the cells whose centres fall within the polygon.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ex1 <- terra::extract(mintemp, bt_points)  # data frame, one row per point
ex2 <- terra::extract(mintemp, bt_lines)   # data frame, one row per cell touched
ex3 <- terra::extract(mintemp, bt_poly)    # data frame, one row per cell

# With cell numbers and coordinates
ex3_cells <- terra::extract(mintemp, bt_poly, cells = TRUE, xy = TRUE)
```
:::


## TODO Add example of how to attach the extracted data back to the dataframe


We can also use an sf object as a cookie cutter to mask a raster with `mask()`. Make sure that they share the same CRS.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
temp_masked     <- mask(mintemp, vect(bt_poly))
temp_masked_inv <- mask(mintemp, vect(bt_poly), inverse = TRUE)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(temp_masked)
plot(temp_masked_inv)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Raster algebra

Rasters are a very efficient way to do mathematical operations on spatial data, because the cell coordinates do not need to be handled explicitly.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tempkelv <- mintemp + 273
tempsq   <- mintemp ^ 2
tempdiff <- maxtemp - mintemp

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(mintemp)
plot(tempkelv)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(tempsq)
plot(tempdiff)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-2.png){width=672}
:::
:::


For applying a custom function across layers, use `app()` (which replaces `calc()` from the old **raster** package):


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Mean across layers
temp_mean <- app(temps, mean)
plot(temp_mean)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Moving window operations

A moving window operation (also known as a kernel) is a computation carried out in each cell using values of adjacent cells.

Let's calculate the variability of bottom depth using two window sizes.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Get depth data
xlim <- c(-28, -10)
ylim <- c(62.5, 67.5)

depth <-
  getNOAA.bathy(lon1 = xlim[1], lon2 = xlim[2],
                lat1 = ylim[1], lat2 = ylim[2],
                resolution = 1, keep = TRUE, path="./data/") |> 
  marmap::as.raster() |>    # marmap still returns a raster object
  rast()                    # convert to terra SpatRaster

writeRaster(depth, "./data/Iceland_depth.tif", overwrite = TRUE)

depth[depth > 0] <- NA  # Ignore land

# Small scale depth variability: 3x3 window
depth_var_ss <- focal(depth,
                      w = matrix(1/9, ncol=3, nrow = 3),
                      fun = var,
                      na.rm = TRUE)

# Large scale depth variability: 15x15 window
depth_var_ls <- focal(depth,
                      w = matrix(1/225, ncol=15, nrow = 15),
                      fun = var,
                      na.rm = TRUE)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(depth_var_ss, zlim = c(0, 1000))
plot(depth_var_ls)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Zonal statistics

Zonal statistics refers to the calculation of statistics on values of a raster within the zones of another raster. Both rasters need to have the same geometry.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Near-bottom current speeds
current_sp <- rast("./data/Iceland_currentsp.tif")

# Classification matrix
cm <- matrix(c(
     -400, 0, 1,
     -700, -400, 2,
     -2500, -700, 3),
  ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)

depth_reclass <- classify(depth, cm)

# Make sure rasters have the same geometry
compareGeom(depth, current_sp)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] TRUE
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Mean current speed per depth zone (replaces zonal())
zonal(current_sp, depth_reclass, fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  layer Iceland_currentsp
1     1        0.05107170
2     2        0.08771757
3     3        0.06289445
```


:::
:::


# Changing the geometry of a raster

## Modify the extent and origin


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ex_depth <- extend(depth, c(40, 500))  # Larger extent

or_depth <- depth
origin(or_depth) <- c(0, 0)           # Change origin

ext(depth)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
SpatExtent : -28, -10, 62.5, 67.5 (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
ext(or_depth)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
SpatExtent : -28, -10, 62.5, 67.5 (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
```


:::
:::


## Changing the resolution

`aggregate()` and `disagg()` change the resolution by merging or splitting cells. Note: in **terra**, `disaggregate()` is renamed `disagg()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
res(depth)   # Resolution
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0.01666667 0.01666667
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
dim(depth)   # Rows, columns, layers
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1]  300 1080    1
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
agg_depth <- aggregate(depth, fact = 5, fun = mean)
dim(agg_depth)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1]  60 216   1
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
dis_depth <- disagg(depth, fact = 5, method = "bilinear")
dim(dis_depth)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 1500 5400    1
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(depth)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(agg_depth)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-2.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(dis_depth)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-3.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Cropping


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ext_obj    <- ext(c(-25, -20, 63, 65))
crop_depth <- crop(depth, ext_obj)
plot(crop_depth)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-22-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Merging rasters


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ice_temp <- mintemp + 10

# merge(): overlapping cells use values from the first raster
icefar_temp <- merge(ice_temp, far_temp)
plot(icefar_temp)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# mosaic(): overlapping cells are combined using a function
icefar_temp2 <- mosaic(ice_temp, far_temp, fun = "mean")
plot(icefar_temp2)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-23-2.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Resampling and projecting rasters

**Resampling** transfers values between rasters with different origin or resolution. **Projecting** additionally changes the coordinate reference system. In **terra** both are handled by `resample()` and `project()` (the latter replaces `projectRaster()`).


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Create a target raster with a shifted origin and finer resolution
target1 <- rast(depth)
origin(target1) <- c(0, 0)
res(target1)    <- 0.025

depth_res <- resample(depth, target1, method = "bilinear")

# Changing the CRS (replaces projectRaster())
crs(depth)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "GEOGCRS[\"unknown\",\n    DATUM[\"World Geodetic System 1984\",\n        ELLIPSOID[\"WGS 84\",6378137,298.257223563,\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]],\n        ID[\"EPSG\",6326]],\n    PRIMEM[\"Greenwich\",0,\n        ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433],\n        ID[\"EPSG\",8901]],\n    CS[ellipsoidal,2],\n        AXIS[\"longitude\",east,\n            ORDER[1],\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433,\n                ID[\"EPSG\",9122]]],\n        AXIS[\"latitude\",north,\n            ORDER[2],\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433,\n                ID[\"EPSG\",9122]]]]"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
crs(mintemp)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "PROJCRS[\"unnamed\",\n    BASEGEOGCRS[\"WGS 84\",\n        DATUM[\"World Geodetic System 1984\",\n            ELLIPSOID[\"WGS 84\",6378137,298.257223563,\n                LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]]],\n        PRIMEM[\"Greenwich\",0,\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433]],\n        ID[\"EPSG\",4326]],\n    CONVERSION[\"Lambert Azimuthal Equal Area\",\n        METHOD[\"Lambert Azimuthal Equal Area\",\n            ID[\"EPSG\",9820]],\n        PARAMETER[\"Latitude of natural origin\",69,\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8801]],\n        PARAMETER[\"Longitude of natural origin\",-4,\n            ANGLEUNIT[\"degree\",0.0174532925199433],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8802]],\n        PARAMETER[\"False easting\",0,\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8806]],\n        PARAMETER[\"False northing\",0,\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1],\n            ID[\"EPSG\",8807]]],\n    CS[Cartesian,2],\n        AXIS[\"(E)\",east,\n            ORDER[1],\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]],\n        AXIS[\"(N)\",north,\n            ORDER[2],\n            LENGTHUNIT[\"metre\",1]]]"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Match only the CRS
depth_proj1 <- project(depth, crs(mintemp))

# Complete raster alignment (CRS + geometry)
depth_proj2 <- project(depth, mintemp)
```
:::


Use `method = "bilinear"` for continuous rasters and `method = "near"` (nearest neighbour) for categorical or integer rasters.

# Rasterization

Rasterization converts vector data into a raster.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# A target raster from scratch
target <- rast(xmin = -28, xmax = -10,
               ymin = 62.5, ymax = 67.5,
               res = 0.2,
               crs = "EPSG:4326")

stations <- read_csv("./data/is_smb_stations.csv") |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon1", "lat1"), crs = 4326)

# Rasterize: count of stations per cell
station_rst1 <- rasterize(stations, target, fun = "count")

# Rasterize: sum of a field
station_rst2 <- rasterize(stations, target,
                           field = "duration", fun = "sum")

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(station_rst1, main = "Number of stations")
plot(vect(stations), add = TRUE, pch = ".")
plot(station_rst2, main = "Total towing time")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Rasterizing polygons:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nfu <- read_sf("./data/nephrops_fu.gpkg") %>% # Note the tidyverse pipe!
  mutate(area = units::set_units(st_area(.), km^2))

target <- rast(xmin = -15, xmax = 13,
               ymin = 36.5, ymax = 62,
               res = 0.5,
               crs = "EPSG:4326")

# Rasterize by presence/absence
nfu_rst <- rasterize(nfu, target)

# Rasterize by polygon name
nfu_names_rst <- rasterize(nfu, target, field="name")

# Rasterize by polygon area
nfu_areas_rst <- rasterize(nfu, target, field="area")

plot(nfu_rst)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-26-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(nfu_names_rst)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-26-2.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(nfu_areas_rst)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-26-3.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Rasters and ggplot

The **tidyterra** package provides `geom_spatraster()`, which lets you plot SpatRaster objects directly with ggplot without first converting them to a data frame. This is the recommended approach.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
depth <- rast("./data/Iceland_depth.tif")

coast <- read_sf("./data/iceland_coastline.gpkg") |>
  st_transform(4326)

ggplot() +
  geom_spatraster(data = depth) +
  geom_sf(data = coast) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(na.value = "transparent") +
  theme_bw()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-27-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


If you prefer to work with a data frame (for example to use `geom_raster()`), you can still convert:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
depth.df <- as.data.frame(depth, xy = TRUE)
head(depth.df)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
          x        y     layer
1 -27.99167 67.49167 -302.6884
2 -27.97500 67.49167 -304.0883
3 -27.95833 67.49167 -304.7184
4 -27.94167 67.49167 -303.7683
5 -27.92500 67.49167 -301.7484
6 -27.90833 67.49167 -304.7283
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = depth.df, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = layer)) +
  geom_sf(data = coast) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(na.value = "transparent") +
  theme_bw()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


`geom_spatraster()` is generally preferable for large rasters because it sub-samples intelligently to match screen resolution, while `geom_raster()` will attempt to render every cell.

# The **stars** package

The **stars** package provides datacubes — dense arrays for storing spatio-temporal data where space (2D or 3D) and time are the array dimensions. It is not a replacement for **terra** but complements it, and it plays well with the tidyverse.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(stars)

depth.st <- st_as_stars(depth)

ggplot() +
  geom_stars(data = depth.st) +
  geom_sf(data = coast) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(na.value = "transparent")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](rasters_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-29-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


For more on **stars**: https://r-spatial.github.io/stars/
